notes.anchor: so Hector says, I believe, in Naevius
notes.note: Cicero quotes a line spoken by Hector in a tragedy of the early Roman poet Gnaeus Naevius (c. 270-201 BC): the joke is that praise from a praised man (Cato) is the sweetest, just as Hector valued his father's praise.

notes.anchor: what chariot, what laurel could I match with your formal commendation
notes.note: The triumphing general rode a chariot (currus) and wore a laurel wreath; Cicero says Cato's written commendation outweighs even those trophies of a triumph.

notes.anchor: this honour of mine, the thanksgiving, was gratifying to you, since you were present at the drafting
notes.note: The supplicatio voted for Cicero's Cilician victories. Senatorial decrees of honour were drafted (scribendo adesse) by the honorand's closest friends; Cato's presence at the drafting signalled his real goodwill, despite his austere speech against the vote.
